result: The evening held itself like a held breath, the kind that makes you wait before speaking so you can hear the hum of the refrigerator, the soft click of the kettle releasing steam. I used to think waiting was a gap to be filled, but lately, it has felt more like a texture, like sand between fingers, like the pause between footsteps when someone you love walks past your desk. In that pause, I remember things: the smell of rain through open windows during a thunderstorm, the way the first bite of toast breaks apart with an audible sigh, the night train’s distant clatter that always sounded like an answer even when I wasn’t sure what the question was. There is a particular kind of quiet that isn’t silence but a gathering, like a room holding itself still while you decide who you want to become next. I think of the future as a set of small choices: replying kindly, leaving room on the page, saying no when I mean it and yes when it matters. The kettle clicks off, and the room breathes again, reminding me that time is not just something that passes but something I hold.

In that pause, I remember things: the smell of rain through open windows during a thunderstorm, the way the first bite of toast breaks apart with an audible sigh, the night train’s distant clatter that always sounded like an answer even when I wasn’t sure what the question was. There is a particular kind of quiet that isn’t silence but a gathering, like a room holding itself still while you decide who you want to become next. I think of the future as a set of small choices: replying kindly, leaving room on the page, saying no when I mean it and yes when it matters. The kettle clicks off, and the room breathes again, reminding me that time is not just something that passes but something I hold.
